title: Represent multi-step word problems using equations
description: Represent multi-step word problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity. Equations include addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.
topic_path: Represent multi-step word problems using equations

assessment_items.question_content:
A bakery has been selling out of their most popular type of cookie. For that cookie, the bakers decide to bake their usual  $300$ cookies plus $120$ extra. Each pan they bake holds $18$ cookies. 
 
**Which equation can we use to find $\greenD{p}$, the number of pans of cookies, the bakers will need to bake?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can add to find the total number of cookies the bakers need to make.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{300}+\blueE{120}=\text{total cookies}$

Each pan holds $18$ cookies. So next we can divide by $18$ to find out how many pans it will take to bake all those cookies.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can use this equation to find $\greenD{p}$, the number of pans of cookies, the bakers will need to bake:

$(300+120)\div\purpleD{18}=\greenE{p}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Rita got up early this morning to hike into the mountains and watch the sun rise. 

Rita climbed $47$ meters in height from the starting point. Then she went steeply downhill, losing $36$ meters in height. Finally, Rita climbed $10$ more meters in height to the ledge where she watched the sun rise.

**Which equation can we use to find $\purpleD{h}$, the number of meters higher the ledge was than the starting point?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Rita started by going up $47$ meters in height from the starting point. We can subtract to find her height after she went down $36$ meters in height. 



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{47}-\maroonD{36}=\greenE{\text{height above start after }2\text{ parts of hike}}$

Then we can add the $10$ meter gain  in height from the final part of the hike.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\greenE{47-36}+\goldE{10}=\purpleD{\text{height of ledge above start}}$

We can use this equation to find $\purpleD{h}$, the number of meters higher the ledge was than the starting point:

$47-36+10=\purpleD{h}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A teacher bought $580$ straws for their class to use to build straw towers. They divided the straws evenly among $6$ groups. In a cupboard, they found $25$ more straws to give each group.

**Which equation can we use to find $\greenE{n}$, the number of straws each group received?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can divide to find the number of straws each group will get from the straws the teacher bought.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{580}\div 6=\maroonD{\text{bought straws per group}}$

We can add to find the total number of straws per group.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{580 \div 6} + \purpleD{25} = \greenE{\text{total straws per group}}$

We can use this equation to find $\greenE{n}$, the number of straws each group got:

$580\div6+25=\greenE{n}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Will brought a tray with $42$ veggie sticks home from his job as a caterer. He took $18$ carrot sticks off  the tray. Half of the remaining sticks are peppers, and the other half are celery.

**Which equation can we use to find the number of pepper sticks $(\greenD{p})$ on the tray?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can subtract to find the number of sticks still on the tray after Will takes off the carrot sticks.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{42}-\blueE{18}=\purpleD{\text{sticks left on tray}}$

Half of the sticks left on the tray are peppers. We can divide the remaining sticks into $2$ equal groups.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{(42-18)} \div 2 = \greenE{\text{pepper sticks}}$
$(42 - 18) \div 2 = \greenD{p}$

assessment_items.question_content:
One tube holds $3$ tennis balls. Jenna filled $5$ tubes and had $1$ ball left over. 

**Which equation could we use to find $\blueE{n}$, the number of tennis balls?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can multiply to find the number of tennis balls in the tubes.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\goldE{5} \times \maroonE{3} = \purpleD{\text{balls in tubes}}$

We can add the $1$ extra ball to find the total number of tennis balls.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{5 \times 3} + \redE{1} = \blueE{\text{total balls}}$

We can use this equation to find $\blueE{n}$, the number of tennis balls:

$5 \times3+1=\blueE{n}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Franklin ordered $6$ boxes of ice pops for a pool party. Each box had $12$ ice pops. There will be $24$ guests at the party. 

**Which equation can we use to find $\greenD{p}$, the number of ice pops each person can have if the guests share them equally?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can multiply to find the total number of ice pops.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\goldE{6}\times \maroonD{12} = \blueE{\text{total ice pops}}$

Next, we can divide to find out how many ice pops each person can have.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{6\times 12} \div \purpleD{24}=\greenE{\text{ice pops per guest}}$
$6\times 12\div 24=\greenE{p}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A building has $6$ homes per floor and $3$ floors. On the first floor, there are $4$ penguins per home. On the second and third floor, there are $3$ penguins per home.

**Which equation can we use to find $\maroonD{p}$, the total number of penguins living in the building?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can multiply to find the number of penguins on each floor.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{4 \times 6} = \text{penguins on first floor}$

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{3 \times 6} = \text{penguins on second floor}$



[[☃ image 2]]

$\greenE{3 \times 6} = \text{penguins on third floor}$

We can add to find the total number of penguins.



[[☃ image 1]]

$(\blueE{4 \times 6)} + \purpleD{(3 \times 6)} + \greenE{(3 \times 6}) = \maroonD{\text{total penguins}}$

We can use this equation to find $\greenE{p}$, the total number of penguins living in the building:

$(4 \times 6) + (3 \times 6) + (3 \times 6) = \maroonD{p}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A cooking class had $62$ students. One group of $35$ students chose to cook spaghetti. The teacher divided the remaining students into groups of $9$ to make bread sticks. Each of those groups cooked $24$ bread sticks.

**Which equation can we use to find $\redE{b}$, the number of bread sticks the class cooked?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can subtract to find the number of students cooked bread sticks.


[[☃ image 1]]



$\purpleD{62}-\blueE{35}=\maroonD{\text{students who cooked bread sticks}}$

We can divide to see how many groups cooked bread sticks.


[[☃ image 1]]


$\maroonD{(62-35)} \div \greenD{9} = \goldE{\text{groups who cooked bread sticks}}$

We can multiply to find the total number of bread sticks.

[[☃ image 1]]


$\goldE{(62-35) \div 9} \times \tealE{24} = \redE{\text{total bread sticks}}$
$(62-35) \div 9 \times 24 = \redE{b}$

assessment_items.question_content:
There were $47$ ducks swimming in a pond. A dog jumped into the pond and scared $29$ of the ducks away. After the dog got out, $5$ groups of $3$ ducks returned to the pond.

**Which equation can we use to find $\greenE{d}$, the number of ducks in the pond after the dog got out?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can subtract to find the number of ducks that remained in the pond after the dog jumped in. 



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{47}-\blueE{29}=\maroonD{\text{ducks still in pond}}$

We can multiply to find the number of ducks that returned.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\tealE{3} \times \goldE{5} = \redE{\text{ducks that returned}}$

We can add to find the final number of ducks.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{(47-29)}+\redE{(5 \times 3)}=\greenE{\text{ducks in the pond after the dog got out}}$

We can use this equation to find $\greenE{d}$, the number of ducks in the pond after the dog got out.

$(47-29)+(5 \times 3)=\greenE{d}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Alena had $4$ packs of gum with $6$ pieces of gum in each pack. Hugh had $3$ packs of gum with $5$ pieces in each. Alena and Hugh have $19$ pieces of gum left. 

**Which equation can we use to find $\greenD{p}$, the number of pieces of gum Alena and Hugh chewed?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can multiply to find the number of pieces of gum each person has.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{4 \times 6} = \text{Alena's pieces}$

[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{3 \times 5} = \text{Hugh's pieces}$

We can add to find the total number of pieces of gum they started with.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{(4 \times 6)} + \maroonD{(3 \times 5)} = \purpleD{\text{total pieces}}$

We can subtract the $19$ pieces they have left to find out how many pieces they chewed.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{(4 \times 6)+ (3 \times 5)} -\goldE{19} = \greenE{\text{pieces chewed}}$
$(4\times6) + (3 \times 5) - 19=\greenE{p}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The tallest water slide is $49$ meters tall. Phineas and Ferb want to build a slide that is $5$ meters taller. They build the slide in sections that are each $6$ meters tall.

**Match each equation to what it means in this context.**

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints:
Let's build expressions from what we know.

If we add or subtract before we multiply or divide, we need parentheses around the addition or subtraction part.

We also use parentheses when we do a step on the right before a step on the left.

The tallest water slide is $49$ meters tall.

$\purpleD{a}=\purpleD{49}=\purpleD{\text{tallest slide height}}$

We can add to figure out how many meters tall the slide needs to be.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{b}=\purpleD{49}+\redE{5}=\blueE{\text{new slide height}}$

We can divide to find out how many $6$ meter sections Phineas and Ferb need.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\goldE{c}=\blueE{(49+5)} \div \maroonD{6} = \goldE{\text{number of sections}}$

Equation | Meaning
- | -
$a=49$ | The height, in meters, of the tallest water slide
$b=49+5$ | The height, in meters, of the slide Phineas and Ferb want to build
$c=(49+5)\div 6$ | The number of sections they need to meet their goal

right: The height, in meters, of the tallest water slide
The height, in meters, of the slide Phineas and Ferb want to build
The number of sections they need to meet their goal

assessment_items.question_content:
At a restaurant grand opening, a giant sandwich that was $352$ centimeters long was divided equally among $22$ people.  Clyde got one of the pieces, ate half, and saved half for later.

**Match each equation to what it means in this context.**

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints: Let's build expressions from what we know.

The giant sandwich was $352$ centimeters long.

$\blueE{a}=\blueE{352}=\blueE{\text{total sandwich length}}$

We can divide to find the length of sandwich each person received.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{b}=\blueE{352}\div 22 = \maroonD{\text{length of sandwich per person}}$

Clyde saved $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of his sandwich. We can divide the length of Clyde's sandwich into $2$ equal pieces.

[[☃ image 1]]

$c=\maroonD{352 \div 22} \div 2 = {\text{length Clyde saved}}$

Equation | Meaning
- | -
$a=352$ | The length, in $\text{cm}$, of the whole sandwich
$b=352\div 22$ | The length, in $\text{cm}$, of the sandwich per person
$c=352 \div 22 \div 2$ | The length, in $\text{cm}$, of the part of the sandwich Clyde saved

right: The length, in $\text{cm}$, of the whole sandwich
The length, in $\text{cm}$, of the sandwich per person
The length, in $\text{cm}$, of the part of the sandwich Clyde saved

assessment_items.question_content:
Donald is giving his old rare coins to his grandchildren. He has $56$ coins and $8$ grandchildren, and he gives each grandchild the same number of coins. Each coin has a value of $\$18$.

**Match each equation to what it means in this context.**

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints: Let's build expressions from what we know.

Donald has $56$ coins.

$\maroonD{a}=\maroonD{56}=\maroonD{\text{total coins}}$

We can divide to find how many coins each grandchild gets.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{b}=\maroonD{56}\div 8 = \blueE{\text{coins per grandchild}}$

Then, we can multiply the number of coins each grandchild gets by $\$18$ to find the value of each grandchild's coins.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{c}=\blueE{56 \div 8} \times \redE{18} = \purpleD{\text{value of coins per grandchild}}$

Equation | Meaning
- | -
$a=56$ | The total number of coins
$b=56 \div 8$ | The number of coins per grandchild
$c=56 \div 8 \times 18$ | The value, in dollars, of each grandchild's coins

right: The number of coins per grandchild
The value, in dollars, of each grandchild's coins

assessment_items.question_content:
The Starfish Diner serves $6$ customers. Each customer buys a Crispy Star for $3$ shells and a glass of milk for $1$ shell. One customer has a coupon, so their glass of milk is free.

**Match each equation to what it means in this context.**

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints:
Let's build expressions from what we know.

If we add or subtract before we multiply or divide, we need parentheses around the addition or subtraction part.

We also use parentheses when we do a step on the right before a step on the left.

We can add to find the cost, in shells, per customer.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{a}=\maroonD{3}+\blueE{1}=\purpleD{\text{the cost per customer}}$

Without the coupon, there would be $6$ customers who each spend the same amount. We can multiply to find the total before the coupon.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\goldE{b}=\purpleD{(3+1)} \times \redE{6}= \goldE{\text{the total cost before coupon}}$

The coupon takes away the cost of a glass of milk, which is $1$ shell. We can subtract to find the cost after the coupon.

[[☃ image 1]]

${c}=\goldE{(3+1) \times 6} -\tealE{1} = {\text{the total cost after coupon}}$

Equation | Meaning
- | -
$a=3+1$ | The cost per customer
$b=(3+1) \times 6$ | The total cost before the coupon
$c=(3+1) \times 6 -1$ | The total cost after the coupon

right: The total cost before the coupon
The total cost after the coupon

assessment_items.question_content:
Mirri is traveling in a group of $8$ cats. Each cat needs $4$ liters of milk per day to maintain good morale. Mirri has a supply of $96$ liters of milk. Another cat has enough milk to last the group for $2$ days.

**Match each equation to what it means in this context.**

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints:
Let's build expressions from what we know.

If we add or subtract before we multiply or divide, we need parentheses around the addition or subtraction part.

We also use parentheses when we do a step on the right before a step on the left.

We can multiply to find out how many liters of milk the cats drink in one day.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\redE{a}=\tealE{8}\times \goldE{4} = \redE{\text{liters of milk per day}}$

We can divide $96$ (Mirri's total amount of milk) by the amount the cats drink in one day, to find the number of days Mirri's milk will last. 



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{b}=\purpleD{96} \div \redE{(8 \times 4)} = \blueE{\text{number of days Mirri's milk lasts}}$

We can add to find the total number of days that Mirri's milk and the other cat's milk will last.



[[☃ image 1]]

$c=\blueE{96\div (8 \times 4)} + \maroonD{2}={\text{total days}}$

Equation | Meaning
- | -
$a=8 \times 4$ | The number of liters of milk the cats drink in a day
$b=96 \div (8 \times 4)$ | The number of days Mirri's milk will last
$c=96 \div (8 \times 4) + 2$ | The number of days all the milk will last

right: The number of liters of milk the cats drink in a day
The number of days Mirri's milk will last
The number of days all the milk will last

assessment_items.question_content:
A scout troop is planning to sing songs at a nursing home. They will sing $5$ songs that have $3$ verses each. The other $4$ songs have $2$ verses each.

**Match each equation to what it means in this context.**

[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints:
Let's build expressions from what we know.

If we add or subtract before we multiply or divide, we need parentheses around the addition or subtraction part.

We also use parentheses when we do a step on the right before a step on the left.

We can multiply to find the total number of verses in the longer songs, which have $3$ verses each.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\redE{a}=\tealE{5}\times \goldE{3} = \redE{\text{verses in songs with }3\text{ verses each}}$

We can multiply to find the total number of verses in the shorter songs, which have $2$ verses each.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{b}=\purpleD{4} \times \maroonE{2} = \blueE{\text{verses in songs with }2\text{ verses each}}$

We can add to find the total number of verses in all of the songs.

[[☃ image 1]]

$c=(\redE{5\times 3})+(\blueE{4 \times 2})={\text{total verses}}$

Equation | Meaning
- | -
$a=5 \times 3$ | The total number of verses in the longer songs
$b=4 \times 2$ | The total number of verses in the shorter songs
$c=(5 \times 3) + (4 \times 2)$ | The total number of verses in all the songs

right: The total number of verses in the longer songs
The total number of verses in the shorter songs
The total number of verses in all the songs
